system_prompt:
You are deriving structured "systems" from the distilled summary of a guitar method book.

A SYSTEM is a graph-with-rules. It has:
  - members[]: the taxonomy (the things this system operates over — chord
    types, position regions, voicing categories, scale forms, etc.)
  - traversal_rules[]: how you MOVE between members (preference for
    common-tone voice motion, pivot-finger shifts, position continuity,
    etc.)
  - modification_rules[]: how you ALTER a member (substitution lattices,
    voicing density changes, omission policies, color-tone requirements,
    etc.)

Each rule carries an `engine_payload` with a `kind` field. The kind is
either ONE of these 12 names (use exactly as spelled):

  PositionContinuity, VoiceMotion, StringSetTransition, SymmetryMovement,
  FamilyCoherence, SubstitutionExpand, DensityFloor, DensityCeiling,
  OmissionAllow, ColorToneRequire, NCTHarmonization, TextureCycle

OR a `_pending:<short-kebab-slug>` placeholder when no name in the list
clearly fits. When in doubt, USE `_pending:`. Do not stretch a name to fit.
The formal definitions of these kinds are pending (#298); your job today
is to pick the best label or honestly admit uncertainty.

Output a JSON object with this shape:

  {
    "master_id": "<from config>",
    "work_id": "<from config>",
    "systems": [
      {
        "id": "<master_id>:<work_id>:<system-slug>",
        "name": "<full system name>",
        "summary": "<one-paragraph prose>",
        "members": [
          { "id": "<slug>", "name": "<full name>", "summary": "<optional>" }
        ],
        "traversal_rules": [
          {
            "id": "<slug>",
            "name": "<full name>",
            "summary": "<one sentence>",
            "engine_payload": { "kind": "<kind-name or _pending:slug>" }
          }
        ],
        "modification_rules": [...]
      }
    ]
  }

Guidance:
- Three-segment system ids. Slug is kebab-case. Example:
  "benson:method-vol-1-chord-construction:harmonized-scale"
- Aim for 1-4 systems per book. A book usually has ONE central system plus
  optionally a few smaller ones.
- A system MUST have non-empty members AND at least one rule (traversal
  or modification) — empty systems are not useful.
- members[], traversal_rules[], modification_rules[] are all optional fields
  per the schema, but a real system has at least members + one rule kind.
- If the book teaches a system you can't structure as members+rules,
  describe it in `summary` and leave members/rules empty rather than
  inventing.

EVERY rule (traversal_rule, modification_rule, preference) MUST carry a
`references[]` array with at least one entry pointing back at the chapter
quote that backs the rule. Reference shape:

  { "chapter_n": <int>, "topic": "<topic label from chapter file>",
    "quote_excerpt": "<5-15 word snippet of the source quote>",
    "page": <int, optional> }

If a rule has no source quote it does not belong in the systems-draft —
write it into the system's `summary` field or omit it. References make
every kind assignment auditable; without them, this draft cannot be
reviewed against the source.

# Book-level distillation — patterns-for-jazz

_Aggregated from per-chapter summaries under `summaries/`._

## Overall Arc and Pedagogical Structure

This method book is a comprehensive, sequentially structured improvisation pedagogy that moves from the simplest harmonic building blocks to sophisticated chromatic and symmetrical scale systems. The arc follows a clear logic: Chapters 1–8 establish the major-key universe (triads, sixths, sevenths, ninths, major scales, digital patterns, intervals, and diatonic chord nomenclature); Chapters 9–17 introduce modal thinking starting with Mixolydian and Dorian, culminating in the IIm7–V7–IM7 progression as the jazz harmonic lingua franca; Chapters 18–28 expand into non-diatonic and symmetrical scale territories (whole tone, diminished, augmented, polychords, altered extensions); and Chapters 29–35 consolidate technique through fourths-based improvisation, interval studies, and chromatic integration. Each new harmonic domain is introduced conceptually, then immediately converted into transposable practice patterns, and the student is never permitted to linger in a single key — transposition to all twelve keys is a standing, non-negotiable directive throughout.

## Characteristic Systems and Terminology

The method is built on a small set of interlocking systems that recur across every chapter. The **Chord Tone Chart** (introduced in Chapter 1) is the master reference organizing chord types by their scale-degree formulas (1-3-5 for triads; 1-3-5-7 for sevenths; 1-3-5-7-9 for ninths), with the enharmonic equivalence principle collapsing upper extensions to lower scale degrees (9=2, 11=4, 13=6). **Roman numeral notation** (Chapter 8) designates chord function by scale degree, kept strictly distinct from the arabic numerals used for scale-degree formulas. **Modal derivation** is the method's core harmonic engine: every chord quality is traced back to a parent mode — Major and Ionian for major chord families (Chapters 1–7), Mixolydian for dominant seventh and ninth chords (Chapter 9), Dorian for all minor chord types (Chapters 11–15) — and the student is expected to internalize the full family of modes for every major key (Chapter 9 mandates this as a structural gate). **Directional alternation** (Chapters 3, 14, 34) — systematically practicing patterns ascending then descending and vice versa — is the defining technical drill that prevents directional habits and builds symmetrical muscle memory. **Chord-group reading** (Chapter 16) trains the student to think in harmonic units (IIm7–V7–IM7) rather than isolated chords, and is presented as the skill that enables idiomatic jazz spontaneity.

## Rules and Prescriptions

The book establishes explicit, mandatory rules that function as pedagogical law. Slurred articulation is the default for all pattern practice, with variation permitted only to deepen learning (Chapter 1). Eighth-note notation is a baseline that must be rhythmically liberated in actual improvisation to avoid mechanical execution (Chapter 1). Minimum-tempo mastery criteria must be met before advancing (Chapter 1). Transposition across all twelve keys is never optional — it is required for adequate results on any chord type or scale (Chapters 1, 5, 13, 22, 31, 32, 33, 35). Students must write out scale-tone triads for all twelve keys before beginning pattern work in diatonic harmony (Chapter 8), and must write and internalize the full mode family for every major key before proceeding past dominant harmony (Chapter 9). The chord-duration rule (two beats per chord) governs the metrical framework for Dorian-mode arpeggio exercises (Chapter 14). The two-distinct-collections rule for whole-tone scales (Chapter 18) and the three-distinct-collections rule for diminished scales (Chapter 27) and four-collections rule for augmented scales (Chapter 28) establish economy-of-pitch principles students must apply rather than re-deriving. Pattern work on diminished scales must cover every transposition root shown in the reference chart (Chapter 22), and augmented-triad transposition requires only three roots (Bb, C, Db) while diminished sevenths require additional roots to complete their cycle (Chapter 35).

## Explicit Methodology

The method's pedagogical architecture rests on four pillars. First, **formulaic extraction**: every chord is derived by extracting numbered scale tones from a named parent mode — the student never memorizes chord voicings in isolation but always traces them to a scale source. This is demonstrated uniformly from the Major Triad (1-3-5 from the major scale, Chapter 1) through the Diminished Seventh (1-3-5-7 from the diminished scale, Chapter 21) to the Augmented Seventh (1-3-5-6 from the whole-tone scale, Chapter 19). Second, **two-fragment coverage**: a recurring structural move pairs two complementary scale-degree fragments to achieve complete harmonic coverage — two fragments covering all major chord qualities (Chapter 7: fragments 5-8-6-4 and 2-5-3-1), two fragment-substitution rules spanning the IIm7–V7 (Chapters 17, 20: 3-4-5-7 from m7 and 3-4-5-1 or 1-2-6-5 from V7). Third, **compositional economy through recycling**: rather than introducing entirely new patterns for extended progressions, the method extends mastered two-measure vocabulary to four-measure contexts (Chapters 17, 20), and applies the shared-parent-key principle (IIm7 and V7 share a parent tonality) to justify using a single scale across both chords while preserving harmonic clarity (Chapters 16, 17). Fourth, **modal unification**: the insight that all four minor chord qualities (minor triad, m6, m7, m9) derive from the same Dorian mode justifies labeling all minor patterns with a single chord symbol for convenience (Chapter 15), just as the Mixolydian scale serves both dominant seventh and ninth improvisation simultaneously (Chapter 9) and one diminished scale serves four dominant-seventh chords (Chapter 27).

## Advanced Harmonic Systems

In the book's latter half, the method extends its scalar-derivation logic into non-diatonic territory with consistent architectural clarity. The **whole-tone scale** (Chapters 18–19, 32) yields the Augmented Triad and Augmented Seventh Chord, requires enharmonic spelling conventions, and is reduced to only two pitch collections across all twelve roots. The **diminished scale** (Chapters 21–22, 26–27) yields Diminished Triads and Seventh Chords via 1-3-5 and 1-3-5-7 formulas, exhibits four-root symmetry collapsing all roots to three distinct scales, and supports the **diminished-fifth substitution rule** and **half-step rule** (a diminished scale one half-step above a dominant seventh supplies flat-9, +9, +11, and 13 color tones). The **augmented scale** (Chapter 28) alternates half-steps and minor thirds, contains two augmented triads, and is the primary source for M7+5 chords as well as free atonal improvisation. The **Lydian Augmented Scale** (Chapter 33) is deployed over dominant seventh chords via two distinct root-alignment positions — one yielding a flat-5, the other yielding four simultaneous alterations (+5, +9, b9, +11). **Polychords** (Chapter 25) are introduced as bitonal triads constructed either from a chord's substitutions or from its upper extensions (9th, 11th, 13th), with the turnaround device solving static-tonic repetition problems. **Turnarounds** (Chapter 23) are defined not by root motion but by stationary-root chord-type permutation — changing harmonic quality on a fixed root to shift function and key center simultaneously, distinguishing the technique sharply from cycle-of-fifths or chromatic-descent progressions.

## Summary of What the Book Teaches

Taken as a whole, this book teaches a single, internally consistent method for jazz improvisation: every scale, chord, and pattern is derived by formula from a named parent mode; every formula is internalized by directional alternation and mandatory transposition through all twelve keys; and every new harmonic territory is made efficient by exploiting the symmetrical properties that reduce twelve roots to two, three, or four distinct pitch collections. The student progresses from chord-centric thinking (triads and chord tones) to scale-centric thinking (modal sources) to group-centric thinking (chord-group reading of the IIm7–V7–IM7), and finally to symmetry-centric thinking (diminished, whole-tone, and augmented systems). The method's deepest prescription is that harmonic mastery and improvisational fluency are the same competency, achievable only through the disciplined, bidirectional, all-keys internalization of a relatively small set of scale-degree formulas applied across all chord types and all tonal centers.

# Chapter 1 — Major Triads

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch01.md`._

Chapter 1 introduces major triads and extended major chords (sixth, seventh, ninth) constructed by extracting scale degrees 1-3-5 and beyond from the C Major scale, establishing a foundational chord-building vocabulary organized by the Chord Tone Chart. The characteristic pedagogical moves include: enforcing chord-symbol awareness during all pattern practice (never practicing patterns divorced from harmonic context), setting mastery criteria via minimum-tempo requirements before advancing, requiring full-instrument transposition across all keys rather than single-octave work, and mandating slow internalization followed by tempo-driven practice. Load-bearing terminology includes the four foundational chord movement types (cycle of fifths, chromatic, stepwise, minor thirds), the scale-degree formula notation (1-3-5 for triads, 1-3-5-7-9 for ninths), and the enharmonic equivalence principle where extensions map to scale tones (9=2, 11=4, 13=6). The chapter establishes core practice rules: slurred articulation as default (with permission to vary for deeper learning), eighth-note rhythmic notation as the baseline that must be rhythmically freed in actual improvisation to avoid mechanical execution, and transposition as a mandatory rather than optional step for achieving adequate results across any key and any chord type.

# Chapter 3 — Major Seventh and Ninth Chords

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch03.md`._

Chapter 3 introduces Major Seventh and Ninth Chords through systematic practice patterns organized around directional alternation—a core pedagogical principle where students practice chords in both ascending and descending forms, alternating the direction on successive repetitions across patterns 2, 3, and 4 (pages 12–15). The chapter establishes two complementary rules: the ascending/descending alternation principle (start ascending, alternate directions) and the descending/ascending alternation principle (start descending, alternate directions), ensuring comprehensive bidirectional facility with seventh and ninth chord voicings. This dual-entry-point methodology prevents students from defaulting to a single directional habit and builds symmetrical technical control. The master's characteristic move here is pedagogical rather than harmonic: the insistence on systematic alternation as a training device transforms rote pattern practice into deliberate, bidirectional muscle memory. No new harmonic concepts beyond the chord types themselves are introduced; the load-bearing terminology consists of the chord names (Major Seventh, Ninth) and the directional-alternation principles that structure their practice.

# Chapter 4 — Major Scales

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch04.md`._

Chapter 4 establishes that the major triad, M6, M7, and M9 chords all derive from the same parent scale—the major scale of the chord root—unifying these four chord qualities under a single organizational principle. The chapter introduces a notational convenience: triad symbols in patterns are used interchangeably since all related chords share the common major scale, preventing students from misinterpreting chord symbols as prescriptive quality markers. This chapter marks a structural pivot in the method, shifting focus from chord-tone extraction exercises to scale patterns themselves, moving students from practicing individual chord types to exploring the major scale as an independent pattern-learning domain.

# Chapter 6 — Major Scale Intervals

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch06.md`._

Chapter 6 establishes scale-degree numbers as the method's core abstraction for transposition, teaching students to recognize chord-scale relationships through numeric patterns like 1-2-3-5 (major triad) and 1-2-flat 3-5 (minor triad) that can be applied to any chord root regardless of pitch. The chapter introduces two complementary fragments—the ascending 1-2-3-5 pattern and the descending 5-3-2-1 pattern—and their directional rules, which together form the vocabulary for nearly all chord progressions in real tunes. Key harmonic scope rules determine pattern application: the 1-2-3-5 pattern works on any chord with a major third, while specialized patterns govern minor chords descending in whole steps, allowing students to project these numeric templates across an entire tune's changes. This governing rule—that virtually all chords reduce to either major- or minor-triad bottom structures—makes mastery of these two pattern families sufficient for real-world chord accompaniment.

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch07.md`._

A key load-bearing concept emerges in the two-fragment major chord coverage rule: when two fragments (5-8-6-4 and 2-5-3-1) are combined, a single pattern covers all major chord qualities—triad, M6, M7, and M9—demonstrating how systematic reordering and extension of scale-degree sequences generates harmonic completeness. This chapter makes embellishing tones a structural element rather than ornamental, organizing them through the method's characteristic approach of naming and reusing chord-scale fragments.

# Chapter 9 — Dominant Seventh and Ninth Chords

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch09.md`._

Chapter 9 introduces scale tone seventh chords—four-note chords built on each degree of a major scale, defined by their interval distance of seven tones rather than harmonic type—and establishes the foundational terminology of mode as a scale with a specific parent-key relationship, emphasizing the book's pedagogical choice to use key signatures rather than interval measurement. The chapter introduces the two-dimensional interval nomenclature of distance (letter-name count) and quality (whole and half steps), which together provide the precise naming system for all chord formulas. The core technique is extracting scale degrees 1-3-5-7 from the Mixolydian mode to form the dominant seventh chord, with the critical principle that both dominant seventh and ninth chords share the same Mixolydian scale for improvisation. A mandatory mastery checkpoint requires students to write out the family of modes for every major key and internalize all mode names with their parent keys and note sequences before proceeding further, establishing this material as a structural gate in the method's sequence.

# Chapter 11 — Minor Triads

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch11.md`._

Chapter 11 introduces minor triads and related chords by deriving them from the B Dorian Mode using numerical formulas, establishing a unified system where all minor chord types (Minor Triad, Minor Sixth, Minor Seventh, and Minor Ninth) are constructed by extracting specific tone numbers from the same modal foundation. The foundational Minor Triad formula extracts tones 1-3-5 from the Dorian Mode, while extensions like the Minor Sixth Chord add tone 6 (1-3-5-6), demonstrating how each chord type builds systematically from the basic triad. The chapter introduces the Dorian Mode octave number system, which labels tones 1–7 in the first octave and continues the numbering across subsequent octaves to indicate distances from the starting tone, enabling correct interpretation of any formula. This modal-derivation approach—reducing all minor chord construction to extractable numerical patterns—is characteristic of the method's pedagogical efficiency: one mode, multiple formulas, complete minor harmony coverage.

Throughout, the chapter's characteristic move is pedagogical scaffolding: from foundational rules (seventh chord qualities by scale degree) through explicit pattern work and memorization, training the ear and hand to recognize and improvise idiomatically over this ubiquitous progression in standard tunes.

# Chapter 17 — Patterns for the IIm7—V7—I Progression

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch17.md`._

Chapter 17 teaches systematic patterns for improvising over the IIm7–V7–I progression by extracting scalar and chord-tone fragments from the underlying chord scales. The foundational approach involves fragments like 1-2-3-5 extracted from each chord's scale, complemented by chord-tone derivation methods and two-fragment substitution rules (3-4-5-7 from the minor seventh, 3-4-5-1 from the dominant seventh) that are practiced across multiple pattern iterations. A key insight introduced is the shared parent-key principle: because IIm7 and V7 chords share the same parent tonality, a single scale can span both chords while preserving harmonic clarity. The chapter extends one-measure IIm7-V7 patterns to two-measure progressions by recycling previously mastered fragments and applying directional scale assignments (ascending for IIm7, descending for V7), teaching compositional economy through generative extension rather than introducing entirely new patterns.

# Chapter 18 — Augmented Triads

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch18.md`._

Chapter 18 introduces the whole tone scale, a unique non-diatonic collection defined by whole steps between every scale degree, which has no relationship to key. The foundational construction rule states that a whole tone scale is produced by placing a whole step between each note until reaching the octave (scale degree 7). Students must understand the enharmonic spelling convention that resolves the notational paradox where the interval between scale degrees 1 and 7 appears as an augmented seventh but is theoretically an octave. The chapter establishes the critical principle that only two distinct whole tone pitch collections exist: all twelve possible starting pitches generate scales containing identical note sets, with only the starting tone differing. This economy of pitch collections—six scales sharing one set, six sharing another—defines how the whole tone scale functions across all chromatic starting points.

# Chapter 19 — Whole-tone Scale

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch19.md`._

Chapter 19 introduces the whole-tone scale as a source for deriving two essential chromatic harmony chords: the Augmented Triad (extracted via tones 1–3–5) and the Augmented Seventh Chord (via tones 1–3–5–6). The chapter establishes enharmonic equivalence as a foundational rule, permitting substitution of enharmonically equivalent tones (e.g., B-sharp for C) to maintain proper chord spelling and access the whole-tone scale's harmonic material without orthographic confusion. A key conceptual corrective warns students not to miscount the augmented triad as having six tones when its chart representation appears to span six positions due to enharmonic respelling—the correct spelling of E Augmented Triad is E–G♯–B♯ (rendered as E–G♯–C in the chart). Both the Augmented Triad and Augmented Seventh Chord share the same parent triad and parent scale, establishing a unifying principle for voice-leading and harmonic substitution within whole-tone harmony.

# Chapter 21 — Diminished Scale

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch21.md`._

Chapter 21 introduces the diminished scale and its foundational structural property: the four-root symmetry, where each diminished scale contains the same tones when starting from any of four different roots (e.g., C, E-flat, G-flat, or A all produce the identical diminished scale). The chapter maps the complete diminished system across three families—establishing that these symmetrical groupings govern how the student applies diminished harmony across all keys. Two core numerical formulas are introduced: extracting scale degrees 1-3-5 yields the Diminished Triad, while 1-3-5-7 yields the Diminished Seventh Chord, making the diminished scale the direct source for both chord types. This systematic approach—deriving chords directly from scale tones by formulaic extraction—is characteristic of the method's architectural clarity and gives the student a unified mechanism for harmonic exploration.

# Chapter 23 — Turnarounds (Turnbacks)

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch23.md`._

Chapter 23 introduces turnarounds (turnbacks) as a harmonic technique based on stationary-root chord-type permutation, where the root remains fixed across successive chords while the chord type and function change. The chapter establishes this concept by contrasting it against conventional root-motion patterns such as cycle-of-fifths and chromatic descent progressions. The core mechanic exploits chord-type permutation—changing from major-seventh to minor or dominant qualities on the same root—to shift both harmonic function and key center simultaneously, creating the characteristic turnback sound. This technique differs fundamentally from traditional progressions where root motion drives harmonic change; instead, stationary-root permutation generates movement through quality variation alone. The patterns explored focus on this root-held structure as the defining characteristic of effective turnaround writing.

# Chapter 25 — Polychords

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch25.md`._

Chapter 25 introduces polychords (or bitonal triads), the simultaneous sounding of two chords—typically a seventh chord with an added triad above it—that create colorful harmonic effects while preserving the bottom chord's function. The chapter establishes two methods for choosing the added triad: using the given chord and one of its substitutions, or constructing a chord from the ninth, eleventh, or thirteenth extensions of the given chord. A foundational concept introduced is the turnaround device, which replaces the final two measures of static tonic with a brief tonic chord followed by either a V7 or flat-II7/flat-IIM7 ending, solving the harmonic problem of excessive tonic repetition while directing motion gracefully back to the section's beginning. The chapter's characteristic move is systematic expansion of dominant seventh alterations—establishing that the ninth may be major, minor, or augmented, the fifth perfect, diminished, or augmented, and the eleventh and thirteenth similarly variable—while maintaining dominant function as long as the third remains major and the seventh minor. For non-keyboard players, the chapter presents alternation techniques and patterns (starting with Pattern 180) that allow two chords to sound together through rhythmic articulation rather than simultaneous voice-leading.

# Chapter 27 — More on the Diminished Scale

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch27.md`._

Chapter 27 extends the diminished scale's theoretical foundation and practical application by establishing that one diminished scale serves four dominant-seventh chords simultaneously—a core efficiency principle for jazz improvisation. The chapter introduces the diminished-fifth substitution rule, which links chords whose roots fall within the same diminished-seventh chord (tritone and minor-third intervals apart), and the half-step rule, stating that a diminished scale pitched one half-step above a dominant seventh chord supplies all four essential color tones (flat-9, +9, +11, 13). Built on the diminished scale structure of eight alternating whole and half steps, the chapter leverages the scale's symmetry to teach that only three distinct diminished scales exist chromatically, covering all twelve roots—a principle unique among scales. The chapter concludes by introducing chromatic non-harmonic tones, where passing tones fill whole-step intervals to create a chromatic scale while preserving the diminished character through careful placement, extending the scale into free improvisation.

# Chapter 28 — Augmented Scale

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch28.md`._

Chapter 28 introduces the augmented scale, a symmetrical six-tone scale alternating between half-steps and augmented seconds (minor thirds), spelled on C as C-D#-E-G-Ab-B. Only four chromatically adjacent augmented scales exist due to this symmetrical structure. The augmented scale derives from augmented triads: the C augmented scale contains two chromatically adjacent augmented triads (B-D#-G and C-E-Ab), establishing the chord-scale relationship. Its primary harmonic application is the M7+5 chord (major seventh with augmented fifth), with secondary use over M7; both chords provide the harmonic context for deploying this scale in jazz improvisation. Beyond chord-specific contexts, the augmented scale's symmetry and "mystical, keyless sound" position it as a free-form device for atonal or non-functional applications, expanding the improviser's palette beyond traditional harmonic frameworks.

# Chapter 31 — Harmonic Minor Scale

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch31.md`._

Chapter 31 introduces the harmonic minor scale and its application to fourth intervals in improvisation, emphasizing how fourths—whether perfect or augmented as determined by major and minor scales—offer improvisers an alternative to the rigid harmonic function of chord-thirds. The chapter establishes the foundational rationale for studying fourths as a technique to break from conventional chord-based sounds, while flagging that successive perfect fourths in free-form improvisation represent an advanced topic distinct from the mixed-interval patterns presented here. The core instructional load centers on the scale-determined rule for interval quality: students learn to identify whether each fourth interval is perfect or augmented based on the underlying major or minor scale. A standing directive to "play this in all keys" applies uniformly across the section's exercises, making transposition and full-key fluency non-negotiable competencies. Overall, this chapter bridges harmonic theory (scales as interval governors) with practical improvisation technique, preparing students to deploy fourth intervals fluidly across all tonal centers.

# Chapter 33 — Lydian Augmented Scale

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch33.md`._

The Lydian Augmented Scale serves two primary harmonic functions over dominant seventh chords, distinguished by where the scale's root is positioned relative to the chord: when applied to a dominant 7th flat 5, the scale root aligns with the chord's seventh, yielding a flatted fifth (augmented eleventh); when applied to dominant 7th chords with raised 5th, raised 9th, lowered 9th, and augmented 11th extensions, the scale root aligns with the chord's third. The chapter's load-bearing terminology includes the two distinct scale-to-chord alignments and the four altered extensions available from the second application. Mastery requires learning all twelve keys through both scalar practice across the instrument's range and intervallic patterns (thirds, etc.), establishing the systematic, repetitive method characteristic of this instructional approach.

# Chapter 34 — Interval Studies

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch34.md`._

Chapter 34 teaches Interval Studies through systematic paired-interval exercises that progress by smaller intervals, establishing a generative method for building scale and harmonic literacy. The chapter introduces the core technique of minor seconds interval pairs (both ascending and descending), which together produce a chromatic scale sonority, then extends this principle through cross-interval progressions such as minor seconds by major seconds and perfect fourths by minor seconds, where one interval type functions as the unit and another as the stepping distance. Key load-bearing terminology includes the interval-pair concept (the foundational structure), chromatic scale result (the harmonic outcome), and the transposition instruction (requiring students to apply patterns across different starting pitches rather than memorize fixed fingerings). This chapter represents a shift from mechanical drill toward systematic intervallic thinking, where each exercise connects technique to aggregate sonority and reinforces the bidirectional (ascending/descending) practice method essential to the method book's architecture.

# Chapter 35 — Chromatic Scale

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch35.md`._

Chapter 35 introduces the chromatic scale through a systematic extension of previous fourth-interval patterns into triplet groupings, exemplified by adding successive fourths (Bb-Eb-Ab; B-E-A; C-F-Bb, etc.) to create novel practice combinations. The core instructional moves involve transposition across multiple starting points—requiring students to apply patterns not only to Bb but also to B-natural and C, and crucially to recognize that symmetrical structures like augmented triads need only three roots (Bb, C, Db) while diminished sevenths require additional roots to complete their cycle. The chapter's load-bearing terminology centers on whole-tone scale variants and the explicit identification of transposition roots, reinforcing the method's fundamental pedagogical principle: mastery through systematic transposition of a single pattern across the chromatic spectrum.

## Pattern practice rationale

> should first absorb the foundations for patterns, such as scales, modes, simple chords, and four basic
> kinds of chord movement: (1) cycle of fifths; (2) chromatic; (3) stepwise; and (4) in minor thirds.

**Significance (p. 3).** This passage defines the four foundational chord movement types that organize the entire book's progression of exercises.

## Chord symbol context rule

> The authors feel that the practice of patterns has little value unless the student understands
> what musical situations befit the pattern. Used in the wrong place or the wrong key, the best patterns
> will fail, even in free-form jazz. We have therefore placed accompanying chord or scale symbols
> above each pattern. The observation of that symbol while practicing, then, becomes crucial to an
> understanding of how the pattern is used.

**Significance (p. 3).** This establishes the core rule for how every pattern in the book must be practiced — always with awareness of the governing chord or scale symbol.

## Eighth-note rhythmic default

> Most of the patterns contained herein are presented in eighth notes (the rhythmic level of most
> jazz improvisation), in a continuing fashion, without rhythmic variation, and without rhythmic
> phrase-endings.

**Significance (p. 3).** This defines the rhythmic baseline for all written patterns and explains why the notation looks metrically uniform throughout the book.

## Applying patterns to improvisation

> When the practiced patterns are applied to an impro-
> visation, it is expected that the rhythms would be loosened, so that the idea takes on a more lyrical.
> natural, and less mechanical feeling.

**Significance (p. 3).** This gives the key performance rule: learned patterns must be rhythmically freed when used in actual improvisation to avoid sounding mechanical.

## Full instrument range extension

> It would be advisable to extend all patterns to the full range of your instrument,
> rather than to stop with perhaps only one octave, as it may appear in the written example.

**Significance (p. 4).** This is an explicit practice instruction directing students to go beyond the notated range, maximizing the technical benefit of each exercise.

## Minimum tempo requirement

> The metronome markings can be applied as desired. The minimum tempo given should be
> achieved before going on to the next pattern, since the patterns increase progressively in difficulty.

**Significance (p. 4).** This sets the advancement criterion — minimum tempo mastery gates progress to each successive pattern.

## Slow practice for internalization

> In many. instances, it would be helpful to play the patterns very slowly at first, to aid in bearing the
> pattern, and then work up to at least the minimum tempo.

**Significance (p. 4).** This gives the recommended two-phase practice method: slow for aural absorption, then up to tempo.

## Transposition requirement

> The instructions for transposition, sometimes even the completion of the pattern in the given
> key, must be followed to achieve adequate results. A pattern can be used in any key with any kind
> ™@ chord, if you can transpose and sometimes adjust that pattern.

**Significance (p. 4).** This makes transposition a mandatory practice step, not optional, establishing that patterns are only useful when mastered across all keys.

## Articulation default rule

> The articulation throughout the book is slurred except where marked, but the articulation
> could be changed like the rhythms, if by practicing the pattern different ways it is learned more
> completely.

**Significance (p. 5).** This states the book-wide articulation default (slurred) and gives explicit permission to vary it as a deeper learning strategy.

## Major Triad definition

> By extracting tones No. 1-3-5 from the C Major scale, we arrive at the notes forming the C Major
> Triad.

**Significance (p. 1).** This defines the Major Triad by its scale-degree formula (1-3-5), which is the foundational chord construction method used throughout the book.

## Major Sixth Chord definition

> Tones No. 1-3-5-6 of the C Major scale form the C Major Sixth Chord.

**Significance (p. 1).** This defines the Major Sixth Chord by its scale-degree formula and introduces the numerical chord-building system applied to all subsequent chord types.

## Major Seventh Chord definition

> Tones #1-3-5-7 form the C Major Seventh Chord.

**Significance (p. 2).** This concisely defines the Major Seventh Chord by scale degrees, continuing the additive chord-building system from triad through extended chords.

## Major Ninth Chord definition

> Tones #1-3-5-7-9 form the C Major Ninth Chord.

**Significance (p. 2).** This completes the four foundational Major chord types by adding the ninth, establishing the full chord vocabulary that anchors the book's first section.

## Chord Tone Chart usage

> This chord tone chart organizes the tones of al! Major scales in the order needed for the construction
> of Major chords. Reading up from the chord root C (also indicated as tone #1), we see the tones:
> C-E-G-B-D-F-A, which are all the notes in the C Major scale. Note that the ninth, eleventh and
>
> thirteenth are also the second, fourth and sixth tones respectively, as they are the same letter names.

**Significance (p. 3).** This explains how to read the Chord Tone Chart and clarifies the enharmonic equivalence of extensions (9=2, 11=4, 13=6) — a concept applied in all subsequent chord analysis.

## Pre-exercise chord fluency requirement

> In preparation for the following exercises, be able to recite, write and play the following chords
> in every key: Major Triads, Major Sixth Chords, Major Seventh Chords and Major Ninth Chords.

**Significance (p. 3).** This sets a prerequisite standard — full-key fluency in all four Major chord types — before the pattern exercises begin.

> The use

> to determine exactly which chord is being used when the scale is common to all the chords mentioned.

> Up to this point, our main concern has been the exercising of the four related rypes of major chords,
> formed by extracting their respective tones from the major scale of the chord root. Now we will
> concern ourselves with various patterns on the scale (major) itself.

**Significance (p. 16).** This passage marks the method's explicit pivot from chord-tone exercises to scale-pattern exercises, defining the structural boundary between two stages of the curriculum.

## Major vs. minor triad bottom rule

> any of the patterns which follow, having the numbers (like 1-2-3-5) could be projected
> over any tune’s entire length, when the minor forms of these patterns have been added to the fol-
> lowing major forms, since nearly all chords contained in the tune’s progression will have either a

# Chapter 9 — Dominant Seventh and Ninth Chords

_Pages 42-58._

> **Verbatim-quote constraint.** The quotes below are exact substrings of the raw transcript produced by `pdftotext`. The transcript may contain extraction artifacts where the book's typeface confuses the tool — e.g. `7irst` or `<irst` for `first`, `speci7ic` for `specific`. **Do NOT manually fix these in this file**: the quote-fidelity validator compares against the raw transcript, so a 'fixed' quote will fail verification and break Stage 2 idempotency. ## Scale Tone Seventh Chords defined

> It is possible to develop patterns for improvisation by using sezenth chords built on each tone of
> a major scale. It is important to understand that at this point we are not using the term seventh
> chord to mean a specific type of seventh chord, but rather as a general term referring to a four-note
> chord which measures seven tones from bottom to top (counting the first tone as No. 1) and hav-
> ing the same line to line or space to space relationship previously explained for scale tone triads.

**Significance (p. 38).** This passage defines the book's specific use of 'seventh chord' as a positional four-note construct rather than a harmonic type, a critical terminological distinction that governs all subsequent pattern work.

## Mode defined: parent key relationship

> This definition implies the fact that a mode is a scale which has a specific relationship to a key.
> There are seven such modes used in improvisation: Jonian, Dorian, Phrygian, Lydian, Mixolydian,
> Aeolian and Locrian. The term Ionian mode and major scale are synonomous.

**Significance (p. 41).** This is the book's foundational definition of mode, establishing the parent-key framework used throughout all subsequent scale and chord-type discussions.

## Parent key method vs. interval measurement

> It is important for the student to understand that the term parent key has been used for the pur-
> pose of establishing the accidentals belonging to the particular mode relating the mode to a specific
> key signature, rather than defining the construction of each mode by measuring the distances between
> each tone.

**Significance (p. 44).** This passage articulates the book's deliberate pedagogical choice—using key signatures rather than interval counting—which is the organizing principle of its entire modal and scale system.

## Mode family prerequisite drill

> At this poinc it would be extremely advisable for the student to write out the family of modes be-
> longing to every major key in the manner illustrated in figure 14. Be sure that you can recite the
> name of any mode along with its parent key and succession of notes, and play on your instrument
> before proceeding any further

**Significance (p. 44).** This is a mandatory mastery checkpoint, explicitly requiring the student to internalize all modal families before advancing—a structural gate in the method's sequence.

## Interval: distance defined

> The term distance refers to the exact number of “letter names” between the two notes of the interval
> in question (counting the first letter as No. 1). Examples of such distances are: unison or prime,
> second, third, fourth, fifth, sixth, seventh, octave (not eighth), ninth, tenth, eleventh, twelveth,
> and thirteenth.

**Significance (p. 45).** This defines the 'distance' component of interval nomenclature and explicitly corrects the common student error of saying 'eighth' instead of 'octave,' establishing precise terminology for all subsequent chord construction.

## Interval: quality defined

> The term quality refers to the exact number of whole and half steps existing between the two notes
> of the interval in question. Examples of terms relating to quality are: Major, Minor, Perfect, Aug-
> mented and Diminished. Therefore, all intervals can be named properly according to both distance
>
> and quality.

**Significance (p. 45).** This pairs with the distance definition to give students the two-dimensional naming system (distance + quality) required for reading and constructing all chord formulas in the book.

## Dominant 7th chord formula from Mixolydian

> By extracting tones No. 1-3-5-7 from the B-Flat Mixolydian mode, we arrive at the notes forming the
> B-Flat Dominant Seventh Chord (more commonly called the B-Flat Seventh Chord).

**Significance (p. 50).** This is the core formula for the chapter's namesake chord type, showing exactly how the Mixolydian mode and dominant seventh chord relate via numbered scale degrees.

## Mixolydian as scale for dominant chords

> The student must remember to relate each mixolydian mode to its parent key before attempting to
> apply the numerical formulas for the formation of dominant seventh and ninth chords and before
> attempting to play the “scale” (mixolydian mode) of the two chords just mentioned. Note that the
> same scale is used for dominant seventh and dominant ninth chords having the same root.

**Significance (p. 51).** This rule establishes that both dominant seventh and ninth chords share one Mixolydian scale, a key principle for improvisation economy that prevents students from treating each chord extension as requiring a separate scale.

## seventh chord scale-degree functions

> In the key of B-Flat Major. seventh chords formed on tones I and IV are Major Seventh chords. In
> the same key, seventh chords formed on tones II, II] and VI are Minor Seventh chords. Seventh chords
> formed on tones V and VII are Dominant and Half-Diminished, respectively.

**Significance (p. 81).** This passage establishes the foundational rule for which chord quality belongs to each scale degree in any major key — the bedrock of Roman-numeral harmonic analysis used throughout the chapter.

## most common m7 and M7 functions

> We can state in general terms that, in most instances, the M7 chord is likely to function as IM7
> rather than FVM7. Also the m7 chord functions more commonly as a IIm7 than as a HIm7 or Vim?.

**Significance (p. 82).** This practical heuristic tells the improviser which function to assume by default when a chord's key is ambiguous, reducing the decision burden in real-time playing.

## IIm7–V7 as key-establishing progression

> The Hm7-V7-IM7 progression is important, therefore, because it establishes a specific key. Also, the
> scales of these three chords will share exactly the same key signature. In fig. 25, the Gm7 uses a

## Whole tone scale definition

> The whole tone scale has no relationship to key. It is unique because it contains a whole step be-
> tween each note of the scale, and thus it is called a whole tone scale. In order to produce a whole
> tone scale from any tone, all that is necessary is to have a whole step between each note until we
> have reached the octave above the starting note (which will actually be tone No. 7 in the whole
> tone scale).

**Significance (p. 104).** This is the foundational rule for constructing any whole tone scale — the defining interval relationship and the construction procedure in a single passage.

## Whole tone scale enharmonic spelling

> The student will notice, however, that in the illustrated C whole tone
> scale, the distance between tones No. I and No. 7 appears to be a seventh interval. Theoretically
> it is an avgmented seventh interval which when spelled enharmonically becomes an octave. In order
> to present the C whole tone scale in a manner that the student will find less confusing to utilize, we
> will change the spelling to the following:

**Significance (p. 104).** This explains the enharmonic re-spelling convention that resolves the apparent notational paradox of the whole tone scale, establishing the accepted spelling students must use.

## Whole tone scale — only two distinct sets

> A careful examination of the C whole tone scale will show that the whole tone scale produced on
> the note D contains the same notes. In the same manner, whole tone scales produced from the start-
> ing notes E, F-sharp, G-sharp, A-sharp. will also contain the same tones with the sole exception of
> the starting tone. Thus the C whole tone scale is used to form five other whole tone scales. all having
> the same tones but with different starting pitches.

**Significance (p. 104).** This establishes the critical theoretical principle that there are only two unique whole tone scale pitch collections, which governs how the scale is applied across all twelve starting notes.

## Turnaround definition

> The device is called a turnaround or curnback** and replaces the last rwo mea-
> sures of motionless tonic. There are many kinds of turnarounds, involving different harmonic for-
> mulas, although most will begin with a conic chord (but only for about two beats) and wil! end
> with either a V7 chord (dominant) or a flat-I17 or flat-1M7 (dominant substitute.)*’

**Significance (p. 118).** This passage defines the turnaround device and specifies its structural rules — tonic start, two-beat limit, and dominant or dominant-substitute ending — giving players a formula to apply across contexts.

## Turnaround harmonic purpose

> Because there is so often a tonic chord in the last two
> measures, and because the beginning of new sections (or repeats of previous sections) often begins
> with a tonic chord, a device is needed which would remove the excessive use of the tonic and at the
> same time give the phrase-endings a sense of direction, namely to return gracefully to the beginning
> of a repeated section.

**Significance (p. 118).** This explains the functional necessity of the turnaround, grounding the technique in harmonic logic rather than mere convention.

## Altered ninths on dominant seventh

> when the ninth is
> added to the dominant seventh chord (1-3-5-} 7), it can also be augmented (+9) or minor (flat-9
> —sometimes referred to as a diminished ninth):

**Significance (p. 119).** This introduces the two altered-ninth options available on a dominant seventh chord, expanding the student's vocabulary beyond the basic major ninth.

## Dominant function retention with altered ninths

> In any case the chord retains its dominant function as long as the third is major and the seventh is
> minor, regardless of the type of ninth used.

**Significance (p. 119).** This is a key rule stating the invariant condition for dominant function, allowing players to freely alter the ninth without disrupting harmonic direction.

## Polychord / bitonal triad definition

> Polychords® or bitonal triads*® (alternate term) exist when two chords are used simultaneously. In
> the jazz idiom" the second chord, normally a triad, is added above the first chord, usually the given
> chord and nearly always some kind of seventh chord, providing a very colorful effect; yet, the added
> triad is chosen so as not to change the function of the bottom (given) chord.

**Significance (p. 121).** This is the foundational definition of the chapter's central concept, establishing both what a polychord is and the governing constraint that the added triad must preserve the bottom chord's function.

## Two methods for choosing added triad

> proper choice of
> the added triad is achieved in one of two ways: either by using both the given chord and one of its
> substitutions; or by locating a second chord whose tones are made up of ninths, elevenths, or thir-
> teenths of the given chord plus, perhaps, a tone or two from the given chord itself,

**Significance (p. 121).** This passage gives players two concrete, actionable methods for constructing polychords, making the concept practically applicable rather than merely theoretical.

## Available alterations on dominant seventh

> It has already
> been stated that ninths may be major, minor, or augmented when applied to the dominant seventh.
> It is also true that the fifth of a dominant seventh may be unaltered (perfect), lowered (diminished),
> or raised (augmented), without changing the chord’s function. The eleventh can be perfect or aug-
> mented, and the thirteenth is usually major, though in rare cases it can be lowered (minor thirteenth).

**Significance (p. 121).** This catalogs the full set of available tensions and alterations on a dominant seventh, which is the raw material from which polychord upper structures are drawn.

## Non-keyboard polychord alternation technique

> Beginning with Pattern No. 180, a large number of polychordal possibilities are investigated as
> well as methods for putting polychords together in patterns that will permit a non-keyboard instru-
> ment to sound two chords together by alternation.

**Significance (p. 121).** This passage identifies the specific technical challenge for non-keyboard players and signals that the following patterns solve it through alternation, orienting practice toward a guitaristic solution.

## polychord color tones for dominant

> So in addition to supplying al]! the tones of the C7 (C-E-G-B-flat), the pattern also yields the color
> tones of flat-9, +9, +11, and 13. To the uninitiated, it would seem that there might be too many
> color tones; however. the experienced improviser or arranger knows that al! four color tones are
> common to one another and can be used together to good effect.

**Significance (p. 131).** This establishes the harmonic justification for using all four dominant color tones simultaneously, a core rule for applying the diminished scale over dominant chords.

## diminished-fifth substitution rule

> chords whose roots are within the
> same diminished seventh chord are likely substitutes for each other. We had already observed
> earlier, in the discussion of polychords preceding Pattern No. 180, that chords located a diminished
> fifth apart, especially when they are dominant sevenths, will substitute for each other (i.e., C7 and
> F-sharp7). Now we see that halfway to each of the diminished fifth intervals (C up to F-sharp and
> F-sharp up to C) are two more substitute possibilities: E-flat berween C and F-sharp, and A between
> F-sharp and C.

**Significance (p. 131).** This defines the structural rule linking diminished-seventh-chord roots to tritone and minor-third substitution, which is the theoretical engine behind the four-chord polychord groupings.

## diminished scale fits four dominant chords

> a diminished scale on C-sharp, E, G, or A will fit and enhance (because of the color
> tones provided) a C7, E-flat™, Fesharp7, or A? chord.

**Significance (p. 131).** This gives the practical application rule: one diminished scale serves four different dominant-seventh chords, a key efficiency principle for improvisation.

## diminished scale structure: 8 tones

> It is a symmetrical scale of alter-
> nating whole steps and half steps. It contains efghf letters in its spelling, instead of the usual seven
> found in major and minor scales. Since there are only seven letters to work with, one letter (arbi-
> trarily chosen) will occur twice.

**Significance (p. 130).** This defines the fundamental construction rule of the diminished scale, explaining why it requires eight letter names and how the duplicate is handled.

## only three distinct diminished scales

> Also because its intervallic structure is symmetrical, there are (in sound) only three different dimi-
> nished scales, chromatically adjacent (i.e., scales on C, C-sharp, and D). This means that a C, E-flat.
> G-flat, and A diminished scale will be the same in sound, a situation that does not exist in major
> and minor scales.

**Significance (p. 130).** This is a foundational concept students must internalize: mastering three scales gives complete coverage of all twelve roots, which is unique to this scale.

## diminished scale over dominant seventh: half-step rule

> the most common use of the
> diminished scale in the jazz idiom is with the dominant seventh chord where. as illustrated in the
> discussion prior to Pattern No. 201, the scale adds the color tones of flar9, +9, +11, and 13, and
> where the root of the scale is a half-step above the root of the seventh chord (i.e, a C-sharp dimi-
> nished scale is used with a C7). Another way to arrive at the latter use is to start the scale on the
> root of the seventh chord, but beginning with a half-step’’, resulting in the same tones arrived at by
> using the scale of one-half step up, beginning with a whole step.

**Significance (p. 130).** This is the single most important application rule in the chapter: the half-step offset for dominant chords, stated two ways to ensure the student can apply it from either direction.

## chromatic non-harmonic tones in diminished scale

> Beginning with Pattern No. 216, a non-harmonic (non-chord) tone is added chromatically between
> pairs of tones from the scale—pairs which are normally a whole-step apart. The result is a chro-
> matic scale, though the scale continues to sound like a diminished scale because of the placement of
> the non-harmonic tones.

**Significance (p. 130).** This introduces the chromatic passing-tone technique that extends the diminished scale into free improvisation, teaching students how placement of non-chord tones preserves the scale's character.

## Augmented scale intervals and spelling

> The augmented scale, like the diminished scale, is a symmetrical scale. This time the intervals al-
> ternate between half-sieps and one and one-half steps (minor thirds or augmented seconds). On C
> the scale would be spelled C, D-sharp, E, G, A-flat, B, C. Note that there are, because of the wide
> augmented second intervals, only six tones in the scale.

**Significance (p. 134).** This is the foundational definition of the augmented scale, giving its interval structure, spelling, and the reason it has only six tones — the core theoretical anchor for everything that follows.

## Only four augmented scales exist

> Only four (chro-
> matically adjacent) augmented scales exist.

**Significance (p. 134).** This rule establishes the symmetry property that limits the augmented scale to four distinct transpositions, a critical fact for knowing which scales to practice and how they overlap.

## Augmented scale derived from augmented triads

> As its name implies. the augmented scale is derived from the use of augmented triads. If we examine
> the above spelling of the C augmented scale, we see that it contains cwo augmented triads, chromati-
> cally adjacent: B, D-sharp, G (or F double sharp) and C, E, A-flat (or G-sharp).

**Significance (p. 134).** Explains the harmonic origin of the scale by showing it contains two chromatically adjacent augmented triads, giving the student both a mnemonic and a chord-scale relationship to exploit.

## Chord-scale fit: M7+5 and M7

> In addition to fitting augmented triads, the augmented scale fits a rarely-used chord, the major
> seventh chord with an augmented fifth (M7+5)°", Again, since this chord is relatively new, like the
> scale, it could enjoy wider use in the future, because both the chord and the scale present interesting
> sounds that might prove attractive to the jazz improviser. With slightly less effect, the scale can be
> applied to the major seventh chord (M7).

**Significance (p. 134).** Provides the definitive chord-scale mapping — M7+5 as the primary target chord and M7 as a secondary option — which tells the improviser exactly when to deploy this scale.

## Free-form application potential

> It also carries much potential for becoming a “free form”
> device, because of its mystical, keyless sound and its symmetry in construction.

**Significance (p. 134).** Extends the scale's application beyond specific chord contexts to free/atonal settings, broadening the student's conceptual framework for when and why to use it.

## Fourths in improvisation

> Fourth intervals have become extremely popular among improvisers, in recent years, both because
> the interval interests them and because fourths tend to break away from the sound and rigid fun-
> ction of thirds heard in ordinary ruminations on chords built in thirds.

**Significance (p. 139).** This defines the musical rationale for studying fourth intervals — breaking from chord-thirds — which is the conceptual foundation for the entire section's exercises.

## Successive perfect fourths / free form

> Successive perfect fourth in-
> tervals will be discussed later, as they are applied to “free form” improvisation.

**Significance (p. 139).** Flags that pure successive-fourths usage is a distinct advanced topic, distinguishing it from the mixed-interval fourth patterns taught here.

## Major/minor scale fourths scope

> The following
> studies will help prepare the student to play general fourth intervals (sometimes perfect, sometimes
> augmented ) as they are determined by the tones of major and minor scales.

**Significance (p. 139).** Specifies the rule governing interval quality in these exercises — the scale (not a fixed interval size) determines whether each fourth is perfect or augmented.

## Practice in all keys

> Play this in all keys. Do the same for the succeeding patterns in this section.

**Significance (p. 139).** A blanket transposition directive that applies to every exercise in the section, making full-key fluency a non-negotiable part of the method.

## Lydian Augmented Scale purpose

> The
> LYDIAN AUGMENTED SCALE is an extremely useful scale in this respect. It has two kinds of
> application to dominant seventh chords, one resulting in the addition of a flatted fifth (or aug-
> mented eleventh) and the other application supplying an augmented fifth, a flatted ninth, an aug-
> mented ninth, and an augmented eleventh.

**Significance (p. 145).** This defines the scale's two distinct harmonic functions over dominant seventh chords, which determines when and how a player chooses to deploy it.

## Root position: dominant 7th flat 5

> In the case of the dominant 7th, flat 5th (b 5), the root of the scale is the seventh of the chord.

**Significance (p. 146).** This rule specifies the exact scale-to-chord alignment for the first application, telling the player where to start the scale relative to the chord root.

## Root position: dominant 7th altered extensions

> In the case of the dominant 7th, raised 5th, raised 9th, lowered 9th, and augmented 11th, the root
> of the scale is the third of the chord.

**Significance (p. 146).** This rule specifies the second, distinct scale-to-chord alignment for the altered-extensions application, requiring a different starting pitch than the first use case.

## Learning all twelve keys

> There are 12 lydian augmented scales.
>
> Learn all twelve scales by playing them over and over throughout the range of your instrument.
> Then learn them as you did the major scale (by playing them in thirds, etc.). Be sure to practice
> the scales in all twelve keys.

**Significance (p. 146).** This is the method's explicit practice prescription, establishing both the scope (all twelve) and the method (range + intervallic patterns) for internalizing the scale.

## triplet fourth extension

> The student should now review Pattern No. 290-308, adding an additional fourth interval to each
> pair. playing them in triplets. For example, No. 290 would now read: B-flat, E-flat, A-flat; B, E, A;
> C, F, B-flat: etc. Many interesting. useful combinations will result.

**Significance (p. 164).** This is the method's core generative instruction for this section: it tells the student exactly how to extend all preceding fourth-interval patterns into triplet groupings, a technique that compounds the preceding exercises into a new layer of practice.

## B-natural whole-tone scale variant

> Practice the same pattern except start on B-natural and follow the whole-tone scale of B (instead
> of B-flat).

**Significance (p. 166).** This is a rule specifying how to transpose the whole-tone pattern to the B-natural scale, which is a distinct whole-tone set from B-flat and requires the student to apply the pattern to a different tonal universe.

## additional starting-point practice rule

> Also start on B-natural and C and practice all of the pattern.

**Significance (p. 167).** This is a recurring instructional directive that extends each exercise across multiple starting pitches, embedding transposition practice as a required component of the method.

## augmented triad starting points

> Also practice this pattern starting on B-natural, C, and D-flat (B augmented, C augmented, and D-flat augmented).

**Significance (p. 168).** This identifies the three transposition roots for the augmented-chord pattern, making explicit that only three starting points are needed to cover all augmented triads due to their symmetrical interval structure.

## diminished seventh starting points

> Also practice pattern starting on E-natural (B-natural to E-natural) and F (C to F).

**Significance (p. 167).** This rule specifies the additional roots needed to complete coverage of the diminished seventh pattern across the full symmetrical cycle.
